channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: First house price rise in four months
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK house prices have shown their first monthly increase since March 2026, offering a glimmer of positivity in what has been a subdued property market. However, industry analysts suggest this uptick may not signal the start of a sustained recovery, with only modest growth predicted for the remainder of the year.

A tentative recovery in housing market

The latest figures reveal a 0.3% rise in average property values across the UK during July, marking the first monthly gain after four consecutive months of decline or stagnation. This marginal improvement comes amidst relatively stable mortgage rates and improved buyer confidence following the spring slowdown.

Regional variations continue to play a significant role, with some areas experiencing stronger rebounds than others. The South East and London have shown particular resilience, while more affordable northern regions maintain steady demand from first-time buyers.

Outlook for the rest of 2026

Property experts remain cautiously optimistic about the market's trajectory, though few anticipate dramatic price surges. Several factors are expected to influence the market in coming months:

  • Mortgage rates stabilising after previous fluctuations
  • Ongoing affordability pressures for many buyers
  • Potential impact of autumn government policies
  • Seasonal patterns typically showing stronger activity post-summer

Economists suggest that annual price growth could settle between 1-2% by year-end, representing a more sustainable pace compared to the volatile swings seen earlier in the decade. Homeowners considering selling may find autumn presents reasonable market conditions, while buyers might benefit from less frenetic competition than during peak spring periods.

As the property market enters its traditionally busier autumn season, prospective buyers and sellers would be wise to monitor local market conditions closely and seek professional mortgage advice tailored to their individual circumstances.

item.title: Average size of landlord portfolios grows
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Recent industry analysis suggests the average size of UK landlord property portfolios has increased, with more investors adopting professional business approaches. This trend reflects a growing shift towards limited company ownership structures amongst buy-to-let investors.

Professionalisation of the buy-to-let sector

The private rental sector has seen significant changes in recent years, with many landlords expanding their portfolios despite tax and regulatory challenges. Industry observers note that experienced investors are increasingly treating property as a formal business rather than a casual investment.

Several factors appear to be driving this professionalisation:

  • The growing complexity of tax rules for private landlords
  • Changing mortgage products designed for portfolio landlords
  • Increased competition in the rental market
  • The potential benefits of limited company structures

Limited company ownership on the rise

Property investors might consider that operating through a limited company could offer certain advantages, particularly for those with multiple properties. While this approach isn't suitable for everyone, it has become increasingly popular amongst portfolio landlords.

Key considerations for limited company buy-to-let include:

  • Different mortgage interest tax treatment compared to personal ownership
  • Potential inheritance tax planning benefits
  • Separate legal identity from personal finances
  • Additional accounting and administrative requirements

Those exploring this route may wish to seek professional tax advice, as individual circumstances can significantly affect the suitability of different ownership structures.

As the buy-to-let market continues to evolve, landlords appear to be adapting their strategies to maintain profitability in a changing financial landscape. The growth in portfolio sizes suggests that despite recent challenges, property investment remains an attractive option for many.

item.title: Leeds tops relocation wishlist
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Leeds has emerged as the most sought-after UK city for relocation, according to recent property search data. The Yorkshire hub attracts more than double the per capita property searches compared to its closest competitor, positioning it as a standout choice for homeowners and investors alike.

Why Leeds leads the relocation race

Several factors contribute to Leeds's popularity among those looking to move:

  • Strong employment opportunities across financial, legal and digital sectors
  • More affordable property prices compared to southern cities
  • Excellent transport links including direct trains to London in under 2.5 hours
  • Vibrant cultural scene with renowned universities and nightlife

The city's blend of urban amenities and relative value for money appears particularly appealing to young professionals and families looking to escape pricier southern locations without sacrificing career prospects.

Mortgage considerations for Leeds buyers

For those considering a move to Leeds, there are several mortgage factors to weigh up:

  • Average property prices remain below the UK average, potentially allowing for smaller deposits
  • Competitive mortgage rates may be available due to the city's strong economic fundamentals
  • First-time buyers might benefit from government schemes still applicable in the area

Prospective buyers may wish to compare mortgage deals specifically tailored to the Yorkshire region, as some lenders offer preferential terms for certain postcodes. Speaking with a whole-of-market mortgage broker could help identify the most competitive options.

With its winning combination of economic vitality and lifestyle appeal, Leeds's position as the UK's relocation hotspot looks set to continue. Those contemplating a move would be wise to research both the property market and financing options thoroughly before taking the plunge.

item.title: Residential construction starts drop 31% for last three months
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK housing market has seen a significant slowdown in new residential construction, with latest figures revealing a 31% drop in building starts during the second quarter of 2026. Industry analysts report this marks one of the sharpest quarterly declines in recent years, with private housing developments experiencing the most pronounced reduction.

What’s behind the construction slowdown?

Several factors appear to be contributing to the downturn in new housing projects. Construction costs remain elevated compared to pre-pandemic levels, while higher interest rates have increased borrowing costs for developers. Many firms are also facing challenges securing planning permissions, with local authorities under pressure to balance housing needs with environmental concerns.

The private housing sector has been particularly affected, with some developers pausing projects amid uncertainty about future buyer demand. Whilst the Help to Buy scheme previously supported new-build sales, its absence has left a gap that alternative schemes have struggled to fill completely.

Potential implications for the property market

A sustained reduction in construction starts could have several consequences for the UK housing market:

  • Reduced housing supply may maintain upward pressure on prices in some areas
  • First-time buyers could face fewer new-build options in coming years
  • Rental market pressures might intensify if fewer properties enter the market

However, some industry experts suggest the slowdown may help rebalance an overheated construction sector, allowing supply chains to recover from recent strains. The coming months will reveal whether this represents a temporary correction or the start of a longer-term trend.

Prospective buyers and investors may wish to monitor local market conditions closely, as the construction slowdown could affect availability and pricing differently across regions. Those considering new-build properties might find it valuable to research developers' track records for completing projects during challenging market conditions.

item.title: Landowners advised on unlocking development potential
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Landowners looking to maximise the value of their property assets might consider promotion agreements as a strategic route to unlocking development potential. These legal arrangements can help facilitate planning permission and significantly enhance land value before bringing sites to market.

How promotion agreements work

Promotion agreements involve a landowner partnering with a property developer or specialist promoter who takes on the responsibility of securing planning consent. The promoter typically covers all associated costs, including planning fees, surveys and professional advice, in exchange for an agreed percentage of the land’s enhanced value upon sale.

Key benefits of this approach include:

  • No upfront costs for the landowner
  • Professional handling of the planning process
  • Potential to achieve higher sale prices through optimised development potential
  • Reduced risk compared to pursuing planning permission independently

Choosing the right agreement structure

Landowners should carefully consider the terms of any promotion agreement before committing. The most favourable arrangements typically include:

  • Clear timelines for achieving planning permission
  • Transparent fee structures tied to successful outcomes
  • Flexibility to market the land to multiple potential buyers
  • Protections if planning permission isn’t secured

It could be worth consulting both legal and property professionals to ensure the agreement properly safeguards the landowner’s interests whilst maximising the site’s development potential. The complexity of planning systems means specialist advice often proves valuable in navigating the process effectively.

For landowners with development sites, promotion agreements represent one of several options to consider when looking to enhance property value. With careful planning and professional support, these arrangements can help transform underutilised land into profitable development opportunities.

item.title: EICR Costs and Compliance Checks London Buy-to-Let Investors Should Budget For
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Investing in buy-to-let properties in London can be a lucrative venture, but it also comes with a range of responsibilities and costs that landlords must consider. Beyond securing the right mortgage and finding tenants, maintaining compliance with legal requirements is essential. One key aspect of this is ensuring that the property meets Electrical Installation Condition Report (EICR) standards, alongside other necessary checks. Proper budgeting for these obligations can help landlords avoid unexpected expenses and potential penalties.

Understanding EICR Requirements

An EICR is a mandatory safety inspection for rental properties in England and Wales, designed to assess the condition of electrical installations. Landlords must ensure that the electrical systems in their properties are safe and meet current regulations. The report must be carried out by a qualified electrician every five years or at the start of a new tenancy. Failure to comply can result in fines of up to £30,000 or even criminal prosecution.

The cost of an EICR typically ranges from £100 to £300, depending on the size and complexity of the property. Whilst this may seem like a manageable expense, landlords should also account for any remedial work identified in the report. Addressing issues such as outdated wiring or faulty sockets can add significantly to the overall cost.

Other Compliance Checks to Consider

In addition to EICR requirements, landlords must adhere to a range of other legal obligations. These include:

  • Gas Safety Certificates: Annual inspections by a Gas Safe registered engineer are required to ensure gas appliances are safe. Costs usually range from £50 to £100.
  • Energy Performance Certificates (EPCs): Rental properties must have an EPC rating of E or above. The cost of an EPC assessment is typically between £50 and £120.
  • Fire Safety: Landlords must provide working smoke alarms on each floor and carbon monoxide detectors in rooms with solid fuel appliances. Ensuring compliance may involve installation costs and regular maintenance.
  • Licensing Schemes: Some areas in London require additional licensing for rental properties, which can incur fees ranging from £500 to over £1,000.

Budgeting for Compliance Costs

Whilst these compliance checks are essential for legal and safety reasons, they can add up over time. Landlords might consider setting aside a dedicated fund to cover these expenses, ensuring they remain prepared for any necessary inspections or repairs. Keeping track of renewal dates for certificates and reports can also help avoid last-minute rushes and potential lapses in compliance.

For those managing multiple properties, it could be worth exploring bulk deals with contractors or membership schemes offered by landlord associations, which may provide discounts on compliance services. Additionally, staying informed about changes in regulations can help landlords anticipate new requirements and budget accordingly.

By proactively managing compliance costs, landlords can protect their investment, maintain positive relationships with tenants, and avoid costly penalties. Whilst the initial outlay may seem daunting, prioritising safety and legal adherence is crucial for long-term success in the buy-to-let market.

item.title: Mortgage payment shock to hit millions of borrowers, Bank warns
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Millions of UK homeowners could face steeper mortgage repayments when their current deals expire over the coming years, according to the Bank of England’s latest financial stability assessment. The central bank now estimates that over five million borrowers will see their monthly payments increase by the end of 2028 – a significant rise from its previous forecast of four million last December.

Rising rates set to impact refinancing homeowners

The Bank attributes this upward revision to higher mortgage rates following global economic uncertainty, including geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. Current data shows the average rate for a two-year fixed mortgage at 90% loan-to-value has climbed to 5.32% – approximately 0.75 percentage points higher than in December 2023.

Around 750,000 households coming off pre-2022 mortgage deals this year may experience the most substantial payment hikes. Many secured their current fixed-rate mortgages when borrowing costs sat comfortably below 3%, meaning they could face significantly larger monthly outgoings when switching to today’s rates.

Lenders continue adjusting their mortgage pricing in response to shifting wholesale funding costs and evolving expectations for the Bank of England’s base rate. While the financial system remains robust overall, the central bank acknowledges these changes will create challenges for many homeowners.

Broader financial market concerns

The Financial Stability Report also highlighted potential vulnerabilities in global markets. The Bank noted how concentrated investments in artificial intelligence-related stocks, often financed through increased hedge fund borrowing, have driven up valuations in certain sectors.

This trend has led to greater market concentration in some global indices, with the Bank warning that excessive leverage could amplify risks if investor confidence weakens. The report stated: “Rising equity prices have been driven, in part, by a narrow set of AI-related companies,” adding that this situation warrants close monitoring.

Alongside these observations, the Bank proposed adjustments to capital requirements for major UK lenders as part of ongoing financial system reforms. The changes would give systemically important banks more flexibility in managing their capital buffers while maintaining overall stability.

For homeowners approaching the end of their fixed-rate periods, these developments underscore the importance of reviewing options well in advance. Speaking with a mortgage adviser or checking comparison tools could help borrowers understand their potential new repayment levels and explore available products.

item.title: New online portal aims to end the chase for property sales updates
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Buying or selling a property can often feel like navigating a maze, with homeowners, estate agents, mortgage brokers, and conveyancers frequently chasing updates and clarifications. To streamline this process, The Partnership has introduced a new online portal aimed at improving communication and transparency throughout the property transaction journey. This innovative platform, developed by the firm’s in-house software team, seeks to reduce delays and keep all parties informed from instruction through to completion.

How the Portal Works

The portal allows clients to complete instruction forms digitally, upload necessary documents, and send secure messages to their conveyancers. Homeowners can monitor the progress of their transaction in real time, view outstanding enquiries, and access billing information, all in one place. For estate agents and mortgage brokers, the platform offers dedicated access, enabling them to track key milestones and view updates without the need to constantly request information from conveyancers.

By centralising communication and providing real-time updates, the portal aims to address one of the most common frustrations in the property-buying process: uncertainty about the status of a transaction. This transparency could help reduce misunderstandings and keep the process moving smoothly.

Benefits for All Parties

The Partnership’s managing director, Peter Ambrose, highlighted the importance of keeping everyone informed throughout the transaction process. He explained that the portal was developed after reviewing existing offerings on the market, none of which fully met the needs of clients and professionals alike. The new platform was designed specifically around The Partnership’s workflows, offering greater visibility and reassurance for all parties involved.

Matt Fenn, The Partnership’s sales director, emphasised the significance of effective communication, drawing on his experience in estate agency. He noted that estate agents aim to keep their clients updated, brokers want confirmation that transactions are progressing, and clients seek reassurance that everything is on track. The portal has been built with these priorities in mind, ensuring that everyone stays informed and confident throughout the process.

By fostering collaboration and reducing the need for constant updates, the portal could help streamline property transactions and minimise delays. For those navigating the complexities of buying or selling a home, this tool may prove invaluable in providing clarity and peace of mind.

As the property market continues to evolve, innovations like this online portal demonstrate how technology can enhance efficiency and transparency. For homeowners and professionals alike, such tools could transform the property transaction experience, making it smoother and more manageable.

item.title: Estate agents ‘missing a trick’ on investment properties
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Estate agents could be overlooking valuable opportunities to attract property investors by focusing too heavily on a home's current features rather than its future potential, according to industry experts. A new property intelligence platform suggests that highlighting development opportunities – from loft conversions to full refurbishments – might help agents appeal to a wider pool of serious buyers.

The untapped potential in property listings

Christopher May, founder of Property Stalker, observes that many property listings concentrate solely on existing features like bedroom counts and square footage. While this approach works for traditional homebuyers, it may fail to capture the attention of investors and developers who typically assess properties based on their improvement potential.

"Investors aren't just looking at what a property is today," May explains. "They're evaluating what it could become – whether through extensions, conversions or complete redevelopments. By not highlighting these opportunities, agents might be underselling certain properties that could otherwise generate significant investor interest."

A new tool for showcasing investment opportunities

Property Stalker has introduced specialised reports designed to help agents demonstrate a property's hidden value. These white-labelled documents can include:

  • Extension and conversion potential
  • Refurbishment opportunities with cost estimates
  • Projected post-improvement valuations
  • Local planning activity and comparable evidence

The platform, currently in its trial phase with several agencies, allows estate agents to present this investment analysis under their own branding. May suggests this approach could be particularly valuable for properties where the true selling point lies in their development potential rather than their current state.

"Not every property needs this level of analysis," May notes, "but most branches will have a few listings where the real story is the opportunity they present. A standard description and photos might not do these justice."

Potential benefits for agents and buyers

For estate agents, highlighting a property's investment potential could lead to several advantages:

  • Generating more interest from investor buyers
  • Supporting stronger pricing discussions with vendors
  • Potentially winning more instructions by offering additional services

For buyers and investors, these reports could provide clearer insight into properties that might otherwise be overlooked. The structured format aims to present complex development potential in an easily digestible way, helping investors make quicker, more informed decisions.

As the property market continues to evolve, tools like these might help bridge the gap between traditional home marketing and the specific needs of investment-focused purchasers. While not replacing standard valuations, they could become a valuable addition to an agent's toolkit when dealing with properties that have significant untapped potential.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Estate agents could be overlooking valuable opportunities to attract property investors by focusing too heavily on a home's current features rather than its future potential, according to industry experts. A new property intelligence platform suggests that highlighting development opportunities – from loft conversions to full refurbishments – might help agents appeal to a wider pool of serious buyers.

The untapped potential in property listings

Christopher May, founder of Property Stalker, observes that many property listings concentrate solely on existing features like bedroom counts and square footage. While this approach works for traditional homebuyers, it may fail to capture the attention of investors and developers who typically assess properties based on their improvement potential.

"Investors aren't just looking at what a property is today," May explains. "They're evaluating what it could become – whether through extensions, conversions or complete redevelopments. By not highlighting these opportunities, agents might be underselling certain properties that could otherwise generate significant investor interest."

A new tool for showcasing investment opportunities

Property Stalker has introduced specialised reports designed to help agents demonstrate a property's hidden value. These white-labelled documents can include:

  • Extension and conversion potential
  • Refurbishment opportunities with cost estimates
  • Projected post-improvement valuations
  • Local planning activity and comparable evidence

The platform, currently in its trial phase with several agencies, allows estate agents to present this investment analysis under their own branding. May suggests this approach could be particularly valuable for properties where the true selling point lies in their development potential rather than their current state.

"Not every property needs this level of analysis," May notes, "but most branches will have a few listings where the real story is the opportunity they present. A standard description and photos might not do these justice."

Potential benefits for agents and buyers

For estate agents, highlighting a property's investment potential could lead to several advantages:

  • Generating more interest from investor buyers
  • Supporting stronger pricing discussions with vendors
  • Potentially winning more instructions by offering additional services

For buyers and investors, these reports could provide clearer insight into properties that might otherwise be overlooked. The structured format aims to present complex development potential in an easily digestible way, helping investors make quicker, more informed decisions.

As the property market continues to evolve, tools like these might help bridge the gap between traditional home marketing and the specific needs of investment-focused purchasers. While not replacing standard valuations, they could become a valuable addition to an agent's toolkit when dealing with properties that have significant untapped potential.

item.title: Landlords go bigger as PRS becomes ‘increasingly professional and sophisticated’
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK's private rented sector (PRS) is undergoing a notable transformation, with landlords increasingly adopting professional business models and expanding their property portfolios, according to recent industry research. The shift reflects a broader trend towards commercialisation in the buy-to-let market, with growing numbers of investors treating property as a full-time enterprise rather than a casual sideline.

Portfolio growth and professionalisation

Findings from Pegasus Insight's Landlord Trends report reveal the average landlord now owns 7.3 properties, indicating a move towards larger-scale portfolio ownership. The proportion of landlords describing themselves as full-time or self-employed has risen to 21%, up from 17% in late 2025. Limited company structures appear particularly popular among serious investors, with these landlords typically holding portfolios of 15.3 properties on average.

The research suggests approximately 66% of larger portfolios are now held through limited companies, highlighting the sector's increasing sophistication. This structural shift may reflect changing financial considerations, including potential tax advantages and the ability to scale operations more efficiently under a corporate framework.

Specialist finance needs emerge

With professionalisation comes more complex borrowing requirements. Nearly 40% of landlords with existing mortgages expect to remortgage within the next year, rising to 56% among those with four or more buy-to-let loans. Portfolio landlords appear particularly active in the refinancing market, being almost twice as likely as smaller investors to seek new mortgage arrangements.

Industry experts note this trend creates growing demand for specialist lending solutions tailored to professional property businesses. Larger landlords often require more flexible products, including those designed for limited company structures and complex portfolio financing scenarios. Mortgage providers and brokers able to offer expert advice in these areas may find increasing opportunities as the market evolves.

The changing landscape suggests the PRS is maturing into a more structured, business-oriented sector. While smaller landlords still play an important role, the growth of professional portfolio investors could have lasting implications for housing supply, rental standards and mortgage product development in the years ahead.

item.title: National Landlord Investment Show marks 100th event
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The National Landlord Investment Show is celebrating its 100th event this week at London’s Old Billingsgate, marking a significant milestone for the UK’s property investment community. The event, known for its comprehensive seminars, panel discussions and industry exhibitors, has introduced several new features to coincide with this achievement. These additions aim to provide landlords and investors with a broader range of educational resources as the property market continues to evolve.

New Focus Areas for Property Investors

One of the key highlights of this year’s show is the introduction of a dedicated wealth management programme. This includes a panel chaired by property expert Kate Faulkner OBE, which explores topics such as long-term property investment, retirement planning, tax efficiency and regulatory changes, including the impact of Making Tax Digital. This session aims to help landlords and investors make more informed decisions about their financial futures.

Another new focus is the emphasis on commercial property investment. A dedicated panel will delve into opportunities beyond traditional buy-to-let, covering areas such as flexible workspace, logistics, mixed-use developments and retail repurposing. This reflects the growing interest in diversifying property portfolios and exploring alternative investment avenues in a changing market.

‘100 Interviews’ Series Launches

To mark its 100th event, the show has also launched the ‘100 Interviews’ series, featuring conversations with property professionals, investors and industry commentators. These interviews will be recorded throughout the event and made available post-show on the Landlord Investor TV YouTube channel. This initiative provides attendees and online viewers with access to expert insights and analysis, extending the event’s educational reach beyond the venue.

The organisers have stated that these additions reflect their commitment to broadening the educational content available to landlords and property investors. With over 100 exhibitors and an expanded programme, the event aims to equip attendees with the knowledge and tools needed to navigate an increasingly complex property market.

Adapting to a Changing Market

The National Landlord Investment Show’s evolving programme highlights the need for landlords and investors to stay informed about emerging trends and regulatory changes. The introduction of new sessions on wealth management and commercial property investment underscores the importance of diversification and long-term planning in today’s market.

For those unable to attend, the ‘100 Interviews’ series offers a valuable resource to access expert advice and insights remotely. As the property investment landscape continues to shift, events like this play a crucial role in helping investors adapt and thrive.

The 100th National Landlord Investment Show represents a significant milestone for the UK property investment community, offering fresh perspectives and practical advice for landlords and investors alike. With its expanded programme and innovative features, the event continues to be a key resource for those navigating the complexities of the property market.

item.title: Comings & Goings
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK property sector continues to see significant movement among senior professionals, with two notable appointments highlighting broader industry trends towards flexible working models and franchise expansion.

Veteran agent joins brokerage model

Andrew Farmer, formerly a director at Hamptons for nearly 20 years, has joined Moveli as the brokerage firm extends its presence across the South East. With over 25 years' experience specialising in the Kent property market, Farmer represents a growing number of established agents transitioning from traditional high street agencies to self-employed brokerage models.

Moveli's co-founder Ben Littlewood commented: "Experienced professionals increasingly value the independence of running their own business while maintaining access to national branding, technological support and operational infrastructure. Andrew's appointment reflects this industry shift that shows no signs of slowing."

The brokerage model has gained traction among agents seeking greater autonomy without sacrificing the benefits of an established network. Moveli reports strong demand for its hybrid approach that combines individual entrepreneurship with collective resources.

Franchise network expands leadership team

Separately, property franchise Sourced Living has appointed Nicola Edwards as business development manager during a period of significant growth. The network has welcomed 16 new franchisees this year alone and continues to develop its acquisition support programme.

Edwards will guide franchisees through business development and compliance processes, with particular focus on the company's Assisted Acquisition Programme. This initiative helps franchisees purchase existing lettings agencies and property portfolios, providing support from initial identification through to completion.

"What sets Sourced Living apart is its diverse portfolio strategy catering to modern landlords," Edwards noted. "Alongside traditional buy-to-lets, the franchise supports HMOs, serviced accommodation and holiday lets - giving investors multiple avenues for growth."

The appointments come as the property sector undergoes considerable transformation, with professionals increasingly drawn to models offering both independence and structured support. For homeowners considering property investments or sales, these developments may signal wider changes in how services are delivered across the market.

item.title: Eye-watering cost of Burnham housing plan revealed
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Andy Burnham’s ambitious £39 billion social housing initiative has come under scrutiny, with a recent report suggesting it may deliver far fewer homes than anticipated whilst placing a significant long-term burden on taxpayers. The analysis, conducted by the Centre for Policy Studies (CPS), raises questions about the feasibility and cost-effectiveness of the plan, which aims to address the UK’s chronic housing shortage.

The Proposed Investment and Its Limitations

The plan, which proposes £3.9 billion in annual funding over a decade, could result in the construction of just 14,335 to 15,494 homes each year. This figure represents approximately 5% of the government’s annual housing target, according to the CPS. The report draws on data from the Housing Forum, which estimates the average cost of building a three-bedroom semi-detached house at £251,700, excluding land acquisition expenses.

Comparisons were also made to Sadiq Khan’s Affordable Homes Programme, which achieved a similar number of homes with comparable funding levels. The CPS highlights that while the initial investment is substantial, ongoing public subsidies are required to sustain social housing. Social rents, which are typically lower than private sector rents, often fail to cover the costs of property management and maintenance, leaving landlords reliant on taxpayer support.

The True Cost of Social Housing

The report reveals that the average social rent property generates £5,942 annually but incurs £6,280 in management and maintenance costs. In London, the disparity is even more pronounced, with average rents of £7,380 compared to annual costs of £8,720. This shortfall underscores the financial challenges of maintaining social housing stock.

Beyond construction and maintenance, the CPS also highlights the broader scale of housing support in England. In 2024/25, £32 billion was spent on housing benefit and the housing element of Universal Credit. The UK allocates a higher proportion of its GDP to housing allowances than any other OECD country, reflecting the significant financial commitment required to address housing affordability.

Implications for Taxpayers and Policy

The CPS estimates that social rents are, on average, £10,250 lower than equivalent private sector rents. Across England’s 4.2 million social homes, this represents an implicit annual subsidy of around £43 billion. When combined with direct housing support, the total explicit and implicit taxpayer subsidy for social housing in England amounts to approximately £79 billion annually.

While Andy Burnham has emphasised the need for increased investment in social housing to tackle undersupply and improve affordability, the CPS report suggests that such initiatives may require careful evaluation to ensure they deliver value for money. Ben Hopkinson, CPS Head of Housing and Infrastructure and author of the report, noted, “Many Brits rightly value our social housing stock, but few understand the full scale of taxpayer support it requires.”

As debates around housing policy continue, the findings highlight the complexities of balancing ambitious social housing goals with financial sustainability. Policymakers and stakeholders may need to explore innovative solutions to ensure that housing initiatives are both effective and economically viable.

item.title: Mortgage price war heats up as major lenders cut rates
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK mortgage market is witnessing heightened competition as several major lenders announce significant rate reductions. Falling swap rates have provided banks with greater flexibility to attract borrowers, leading to a wave of cuts across fixed-rate and tracker mortgages. This trend is expected to continue, offering potential benefits for homeowners and buyers alike.

Major Lenders Slash Mortgage Rates

Nationwide is at the forefront of this rate-cutting spree, reducing selected fixed-rate mortgages by up to 0.19% and tracker products by up to 0.12%. Virgin Money has followed suit, trimming rates by up to 0.16% on certain two-year remortgage deals. Meanwhile, BM Solutions and Halifax have also joined the fray, lowering rates by up to 0.15% across their core ranges. Halifax has gone a step further by offering an additional 0.20% discount for Lloyds Premier customers.

The reductions come as one-to-five-year SONIA swap rates have dipped below 4%. Notably, the two-year swap rate has fallen to 3.913%, while the five-year rate stands at 3.999%, down from 4.159% and 4.176% respectively at the start of June. This decline in funding costs is fuelling competition among lenders, with further mortgage rate reductions likely if swap rates remain stable.

Expert Insights for Borrowers

Nicholas Mendes, mortgage technical manager at John Charcol, highlighted the rapid pace of these changes, noting that six lenders repriced their products within 24 hours. He explained that lenders are keen to avoid appearing expensive as remortgage volumes rise. Coventry Building Society stands out as an outlier, increasing some residential fixed rates while reducing buy-to-let (BTL) rates, indicating that the market is not uniformly racing to the bottom.

For borrowers, Mendes emphasised the importance of acting promptly. Waiting for rates to fall further could prove costly, as most lenders allow borrowers to switch to a lower deal if rates improve before completion. This offers protection against potential rate increases while retaining the opportunity to benefit from further reductions.

Advice for Homebuyers

For those considering a property purchase, Mendes advised against delaying decisions in the hope of marginally cheaper rates. Securing a home that meets your needs and budget should take precedence, as waiting for a slight rate reduction risks losing out on the right property altogether. The disappointment of missing a dream home often outweighs any minor savings on mortgage rates.

As the mortgage market continues to evolve, borrowers and buyers may wish to stay informed about the latest rate changes and seek advice tailored to their individual circumstances. With competition heating up, there could be opportunities to secure favourable mortgage deals in the coming months.

item.title: How did house prices perform last month? Industry reacts to the latest data
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

UK house prices showed modest growth in June, rising 0.2% month-on-month according to the latest Lloyds House Price Index. This marks the first increase after four consecutive months of stagnation, with the average property now valued at £299,330. Annual price growth also strengthened slightly to 0.6%, up from 0.5% in May.

Market shows resilience despite economic headwinds

The housing market continues to navigate economic uncertainty, with inflation and interest rate expectations influencing buyer behaviour. Amanda Bryden, head of mortgages at Lloyds, noted that while affordability challenges persist, recent reductions in mortgage rates from their peak levels may offer some relief to prospective buyers.

First-time buyers appear particularly active, with annual price growth for starter homes accelerating to 0.8% in June. The average first-time buyer property now costs £240,433, suggesting sustained demand in this segment despite financial pressures.

Mortgage approvals dipped in May, which industry experts attribute to the earlier spike in borrowing costs. However, with fixed-rate deals beginning to ease from their recent highs, some analysts anticipate a gradual recovery in market activity during the second half of the year.

Industry experts weigh in on market outlook

Property professionals suggest the current modest growth reflects a market in transition. Amy Reynolds of Antony Roberts observes that fixed-rate pricing had been "artificially elevated" as lenders managed application volumes, but notes early signs of rate corrections filtering through to borrowers.

Key observations from industry commentators include:

  • Tracker mortgages gaining popularity as buyers seek flexibility amid economic uncertainty
  • Fixed-rate deals showing gradual reductions, though remaining above historic lows
  • Market activity expected to strengthen if borrowing costs continue declining

Looking ahead, the market's trajectory will likely depend on several factors including inflation trends, employment stability and consumer confidence. While lower mortgage rates could support demand, affordability constraints may continue to temper price growth in the medium term.

For those considering a property move, monitoring mortgage rate trends and seeking independent financial advice could help navigate the evolving market conditions. Lenders and brokers report growing product choice as the market adjusts to the new interest rate environment.

item.title: Summer of speculation awaits housing market
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK housing market may face a summer of political uncertainty following recent policy announcements from Labour’s leadership. With ambitious proposals on tax reforms and housing targets – yet few concrete details – industry experts suggest the coming months could be dominated by speculation rather than decisive action.

Policy proposals spark debate

Recent speeches have outlined potential reforms including replacing stamp duty and council tax with a land value tax, alongside plans for the largest social housebuilding programme in decades. However, without published policy documents, analysts warn these ideas remain theoretical. The lack of clarity comes as Labour aims to deliver 1.5 million homes – a target not yet achieved – leaving many questioning how proposals will translate into practical policies.

Financial constraints may further complicate matters, with reports of a £4.7bn shortfall in defence spending highlighting tough budgetary choices. Former Treasury advisor James Nation noted that any significant deviation from Labour’s 2024 manifesto could raise questions about political legitimacy, potentially influencing housing market confidence.

London market shows resilience amid uncertainty

Prime central London (PCL) has seen prices decline by 3.6% annually for two consecutive months, with transaction volumes down 14% year-on-year according to Knight Frank data. However, the number of offers made fell by just 4%, suggesting underlying demand remains despite tougher lending conditions and political uncertainty.

Tom Bill, head of UK residential research at Knight Frank, observed: “While transaction levels have dipped, buyer interest hasn’t fallen as sharply. The market appears to be in a holding pattern as participants assess the political and economic landscape.”

Concerns have been raised about potential wealth-based tax changes, with industry groups warning that higher taxes on property could deter investment. Foreign Investors for Britain cautioned against policies that might be seen as penalising success, stating: “If Britain signals that achievement will simply lead to higher taxes, many contributors to economic growth may reconsider their position.”

As the autumn Budget approaches, the housing market faces continued uncertainty. With major policy questions unresolved and financial pressures mounting, homeowners and investors alike may need to brace for further speculation before clearer direction emerges.

The lack of clarity comes as Labour aims to deliver 1.5 million homes – a target not yet achieved – leaving many questioning how proposals will translate into practical policies.

Financial constraints may further complicate matters, with reports of a £4.7bn shortfall in defence spending highlighting tough budgetary choices. Former Treasury advisor James Nation noted that any significant deviation from Labour’s 2024 manifesto could raise questions about political legitimacy, potentially influencing housing market confidence.

London market shows resilience amid uncertainty

Prime central London (PCL) has seen prices decline by 3.6% annually for two consecutive months, with transaction volumes down 14% year-on-year according to Knight Frank data. However, the number of offers made fell by just 4%, suggesting underlying demand remains despite tougher lending conditions and political uncertainty.

Tom Bill, head of UK residential research at Knight Frank, observed: “While transaction levels have dipped, buyer interest hasn’t fallen as sharply. The market appears to be in a holding pattern as participants assess the political and economic landscape.”

Concerns have been raised about potential wealth-based tax changes, with industry groups warning that higher taxes on property could deter investment. Foreign Investors for Britain cautioned against policies that might be seen as penalising success, stating: “If Britain signals that achievement will simply lead to higher taxes, many contributors to economic growth may reconsider their position.”

As the autumn Budget approaches, the housing market faces continued uncertainty. With major policy questions unresolved and financial pressures mounting, homeowners and investors alike may need to brace for further speculation before clearer direction emerges.

item.title: Generation stay-at-home: Record numbers living with parents
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research reveals a record number of young adults in their 20s and early 30s are living with their parents, with affordability challenges in the housing market appearing to be a significant factor. Analysis of official data shows nearly 3.8 million adults aged 20–34 now reside in the family home – the highest figure since records began in 1996.

The changing landscape of young adulthood

Current statistics indicate that approximately three in ten young adults live with parents, compared to just one in five during the 1990s. This represents a 40% increase in the number of 20–34-year-olds staying at home, despite the overall size of this age group growing by less than 4% over the same period. Had young people continued leaving home at 1990s rates, researchers estimate around one million fewer would be living with parents today.

The data also highlights a notable gender divide, with 35% of young men remaining in the family home compared to 22% of women. This means men in this age group are over 50% more likely to stay with parents than their female counterparts.

Affordability challenges for first-time buyers

Property experts suggest the rising trend reflects growing financial pressures facing first-time buyers. Analysis comparing deposit requirements with rental costs across England and Wales found that a typical 10% house deposit now equates to approximately 28 months' rent. In major cities, this figure ranges between 18–24 months, while in some London boroughs, buyers would need to save the equivalent of nearly three years' rent.

For many aspiring homeowners, the challenge lies not in meeting monthly mortgage payments but in accumulating the initial deposit – particularly while simultaneously paying high rental costs. The research underscores how these financial barriers may be influencing young adults' living arrangements, with many opting to remain at home while saving.

Those considering their first property purchase might wish to carefully evaluate all associated costs, including deposits, mortgage commitments and legal fees. Professional financial advice could help individuals assess their options based on personal circumstances.

As housing affordability continues to shape living patterns, these findings provide insight into the financial realities facing today's younger generations in the property market.

item.title: Rental yields dip by quarter but up YOY to 7.8%
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Recent data reveals that average rental yields across the UK have dipped slightly in the second quarter of 2023, though they remain higher than the same period last year. Despite the quarterly decline, the overall year-on-year growth suggests resilience in the rental market, with yields reaching 7.8% compared to 2022 figures.

Quarterly Decline in Rental Yields

The second quarter of 2023 saw a modest decrease in rental yields across most regions. This dip could be attributed to a combination of factors, including seasonal fluctuations and changes in property prices. Whilst the decline may raise concerns for some landlords, it’s important to note that rental yields often experience cyclical trends throughout the year.

Market analysts suggest that the quarterly dip does not necessarily indicate a long-term downturn. Instead, it may reflect temporary adjustments in the property market. Landlords and property investors might consider reviewing their portfolios and rental strategies to ensure they remain competitive in the current climate.

Year-on-Year Growth Highlights Market Strength

Despite the quarterly dip, the year-on-year increase in rental yields to 7.8% underscores the enduring strength of the UK rental market. This growth is particularly notable given the broader economic challenges, including rising interest rates and inflation.

The sustained demand for rental properties, coupled with limited housing supply, continues to drive yields upwards. For landlords, this upward trend could provide reassurance that the rental market remains a viable investment opportunity, even amidst economic uncertainty.

For those considering entering the buy-to-let market, the year-on-year growth in yields may present an encouraging sign. However, it’s crucial to conduct thorough research and seek professional advice to navigate the complexities of property investment effectively.

Regional Variations in Rental Yields

Whilst the national average rental yield stands at 7.8%, there are notable regional variations. Some areas have experienced more significant quarterly declines, whilst others have maintained stable or even increased yields.

For example, regions with higher demand for rental properties, such as major cities and university towns, often report stronger yields. Conversely, areas with slower market activity may see more pronounced dips. Landlords and investors might find it beneficial to assess local market conditions before making investment decisions.

Understanding these regional differences can help property owners tailor their strategies to maximise returns. Keeping an eye on emerging trends and demographic shifts could also provide valuable insights for long-term planning.

In conclusion, whilst the quarterly dip in rental yields may prompt caution, the year-on-year growth highlights the continued strength of the UK rental market. Landlords and investors could benefit from staying informed about regional trends and adapting their strategies to navigate the evolving landscape effectively.

item.title: Lack of properties pushing up rents
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Renters across the UK are facing steeper housing costs than anticipated, with many paying above their initial budget due to intense competition for limited rental properties. Recent market trends show a growing imbalance between rental demand and available homes, creating challenging conditions for tenants seeking affordable accommodation.

Rental market pressures drive up costs

The UK rental sector continues to experience significant strain, with property shortages forcing many tenants to stretch their budgets. Industry observers note that prospective renters frequently encounter bidding wars, with some offering over the asking price to secure tenancies. This competitive environment has led to average rental prices climbing steadily across most regions.

Several factors contribute to the current rental squeeze:

  • High demand from young professionals and families unable to purchase homes
  • Landlords exiting the market due to regulatory changes and tax adjustments
  • Limited new rental property construction in key urban areas
  • Increased student demand in university towns

Options for renters in a competitive market

Whilst the rental market presents challenges, tenants might consider several strategies to improve their chances of finding suitable accommodation. Being prepared with all necessary documentation, including references and proof of income, could help applications stand out. Some renters find success by being flexible on move-in dates or considering slightly different locations than originally planned.

Those struggling to find affordable rentals might explore:

  • Sharing accommodation to split costs
  • Looking slightly further from city centres where competition may be less fierce
  • Considering longer tenancy terms which some landlords may favour
  • Registering with multiple letting agents to increase options

With rental market conditions unlikely to ease significantly in the short term, both tenants and policymakers continue to seek solutions to the UK's housing challenges. Renters may benefit from researching local market trends thoroughly before beginning their property search.

item.title: Small investor bank lending down 14% in five years
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Over the past five years, bank lending to smaller property investors has seen a notable decline, dropping by 14%. This shift comes amidst a growing presence of alternative lenders in the market, offering a wider range of financing options for those looking to invest in property. As traditional banks reduce their involvement, these alternative providers are stepping in to fill the gap, presenting new opportunities for investors.

The Rise of Alternative Lenders

Alternative lenders have been gaining traction in the UK property investment market, particularly for smaller investors. These lenders often provide more flexible terms and quicker approval processes compared to traditional banks. For instance, peer-to-peer lending platforms and specialist mortgage providers have become increasingly popular, catering to those who may not meet the stringent criteria of high-street banks.

This trend reflects a broader shift in the financial landscape, where technology and innovation are reshaping how individuals access funding. Smaller investors might find that alternative lenders offer a more accessible route to securing the capital needed for property purchases, especially in a competitive market.

Impact on Smaller Property Investors

The reduction in bank lending to smaller property investors could pose challenges for those who rely on traditional financing methods. With banks tightening their lending criteria and focusing on larger clients, smaller investors may face difficulties in securing loans. However, the rise of alternative lenders could provide a viable solution, offering tailored products that better suit the needs of this demographic.

  • Increased flexibility in loan terms
  • Faster application and approval processes
  • Access to niche products designed for property investors

For those considering property investment, it could be worth exploring these alternative options to understand how they might align with individual financial goals and circumstances.

What Does the Future Hold?

As alternative lenders continue to expand their offerings, the landscape of property investment financing is likely to evolve further. Smaller investors may benefit from increased competition among lenders, potentially leading to more favourable terms and conditions. However, it remains essential for borrowers to carefully assess their options and ensure they fully understand the terms of any finance agreement.

Whilst the decline in bank lending presents challenges, it also highlights the growing importance of diversification in the lending market. Homeowners and investors alike might consider exploring a range of financing options to find the most suitable solution for their needs.

In conclusion, the shift in lending trends underscores the need for adaptability in the property investment sector. With alternative lenders playing an increasingly prominent role, smaller investors have access to a broader array of financing solutions than ever before. By staying informed and exploring all available options, investors can navigate this changing landscape with confidence.
